data.title: Exelon: Why I See Upside This Year
data.description:
We are now five years removed from the S&P 500's peak before the swift and briefly painful 34% COVID crash. February 19, 2020, was not an eventful session, and many investors had turned convinced that equities would largely look beyond the novel coronavirus. That of, course, proved perilous as the biggest bear market (though not long in duration) since the Great Financial Crisis ensued.

Over the past half-decade, Information Technology has soared, the February-March 2020 decline notwithstanding. Among the 11 sectors, Utilities is next to last (only Real Estate is worse). It has also been a fascinating trend change in the Utilities space over that time. The high-yield area is now considered AI-adjacent, but often overlooked is the need to rebuild and reinvest in the US transmission and distribution (T&D) system, and I see upside in one steady T&D utility.

I was bullish on Exelon Corporation (NASDAQ:EXC) back in August of 2023. Shares have returned 11% since then, though that's an underperformance compared with the 35% S&P 500 gain. I reiterate a buy today based on valuation and a sanguine technical chart.

S&P 500 Sector Returns Since the S&P 500 Peaked in February 2020

Earlier this month, EXC reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.64 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast by a nickel while revenue of $5.5 billion, up 2% from the same period a year earlier, was a small $30 million beat. The management team gave FY 2025 adjusted operating earnings guidance range of $2.64-$2.74 per share while declaring a $0.40 dividend. Shares rose by 1.3% in the session that followed as the street broadly liked the performance during the final three months of 2024.

Looking closer at the quarter, EPS came in at the top end of EXC's own guidance range thanks to improvements within its BGE and PHI subsidiaries, helping to offset relative weakness in ComEd and PECO. The firm reaffirmed its long-term 5-7% annual earnings growth target and it anticipates $38 billion of capex spending through 2028-that was up from the last estimate of just $34.5 billion. As it stands, the dividend yield is 3.8%.

Overall, it's steady as she goes, but I would be concerned if capex turned much higher as I would not want to see an equity raise to finance it. Moreover, higher borrowing costs today compared to a few years ago could lead to a higher interest expense.

On the earnings outlook, the growth trajectory is on track. EPS is seen rising between 5-7% in 2025 and through the out year, making the valuation reasonably straightforward. Revenue should increase at an annual rate slightly above inflation while the sell side has turned more upbeat on EXC's profit picture-there have been 11 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days compared to just a pair of downgrades.

Exelon: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume normalized non-GAAP EPS of $2.70 over the next 12 months, close to the current consensus, and apply a 17 multiple (between its 5-year average and the sector median) then shares should trade near $45.90, which is almost 10% above today's price, offering a decent margin of safety for a generally low-beta name.

EXC: A Reasonable Valuation

Key risks include adverse regulatory changes in the utilities industry for the Chicago-based company. I noted my concerns about capex earlier along with interest rate exposure, and natural disaster risk in the Midwest region is an ongoing potential downside catalyst.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an inferred Q1 2025 earnings date of Thursday, May 1 BMO. Before, that shares trade ex a $0.40 quarterly dividend on Monday, February 24. No other volatility catalysts are seen in the near term.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares adequately undervalued in my view and amid a very steady earnings trajectory, EXC's chart is actually quite bullish. Notice in the graph below that shares broke out above the key $41-$42 zone. That rally triggered a bullish upside measure move price objective to near $50 based on the $7 range from late 2023 through earlier this year.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average-it's rising in its slope, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. What's more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart has touched technical overbought conditions, and EXC has pared its big YTD rally just somewhat.

With the near-term 50dma now above the 200dma and with price above both, the pieces are in place for a continued rally from the low under $36 last December. Support is seen at previous resistance, and I expect the stock to climb into the upper $40s this year.

EXC: Bullish Upside Breakout Target $50, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on EXC. I see the $43 billion Electric Utilities industry stock as attractive on valuation, while the chart is bullish.

data.title: 14 Upcoming Dividend Increases, Including A King
data.description:
Welcome back for another edition of upcoming dividend increases. Let's look at some highlights:

  • Dividend King S&P Global extends its 52-year dividend growth streak with a 5.5% increase.
  • Four companies have increases over 10%.
  • The average streak length is 16 years.
  • The average increase is 6%, and the median is 4.8%.

Companies that consistently grow their dividends are often viewed as superior investments over time, reflecting financial health and stability. Regular dividend increases signal that a company is generating strong cash flow, leading to long-term wealth accumulation for investors. This reliability attracts more investors and often drives stock prices higher over time, significantly benefiting shareholders.

My investment strategy focuses on purchasing, maintaining, and expanding positions in companies that meet two key criteria: consistently rising dividends and outperforming a comparable benchmark. I compiled this information as part of my investment strategy and am eager to share my results with my Seeking Alpha audience. This list aims to assist you in making informed investment decisions and building a prosperous long-term portfolio.

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here results from integrating two data sources: the "U.S. Dividend Champions" spreadsheet from a specific website and upcoming dividend data from NASDAQ. This integration merges data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth with their anticipated future dividend payments. It's crucial to note that every company on this list has maintained a record of dividend growth for at least five years.

A company must offer higher total annual dividends to be considered for this list. As a result, a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, but its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories, as I'll use them throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years
  • Contender: 10-24 years
  • Challenger: 5+ years
Category Count
King 1
Champion 0
Contender 8
Challenger 5

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
Brunswick Corporation (BC) 13 2.66 24-Feb-25 2.38% Contender
Radian Group Inc. (RDN) 6 3.17 24-Feb-25 4.08% Challenger
Murphy USA Inc. (MUSA) 5 0.41 24-Feb-25 2.08% Challenger
Penske Automotive Group, Inc. (PAG) 5 2.88 24-Feb-25 2.52% Challenger
Robert Half Inc. (RHI) 21 3.91 25-Feb-25 11.32% Contender
Moody's Corporation (MCO) 15 0.72 25-Feb-25 10.59% Contender
S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) 52 0.71 26-Feb-25 5.49% King
Avista Corporation (AVA) 23 5.24 26-Feb-25 3.16% Contender
Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) 11 2 26-Feb-25 10.09% Contender
Kinsale Capital Group, Inc. (KNSL) 9 0.15 27-Feb-25 13.33% Challenger
CSX Corporation (CSX) 21 1.6 28-Feb-25 8.33% Contender
Brookfield Infrastructure Partners LP Limited Partn... (BIP) 18 5.17 28-Feb-25 6.17% Contender
Magna International, Inc. (MGA) 15 5.14 28-Feb-25 2.11% Contender
Walker & Dunlop, Inc (WD) 7 3.19 28-Feb-25 3.08% Challenger

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date you need to own the stock.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
BC 0.42 0.43 2.38%
RDN 0.25 0.26 4.08%
MUSA 0.48 0.49 2.08%
PAG 1.19 1.22 2.52%
RHI 0.53 0.59 11.32%
MCO 0.85 0.94 10.59%
SPGI 0.91 0.96 5.49%
AVA 0.48 0.49 3.16%
EQIX 4.26 4.69 10.09%
KNSL 0.15 0.17 13.33%
CSX 0.12 0.13 8.33%
BIP 0.41 0.43 6.17%
MGA 0.48 0.49 2.11%
WD 0.65 0.67 3.08%

Additional Metrics

Some different metrics related to these companies include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52 Week Low 52 Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
BC 64.68 62.64 95.13 17.34 4% Off Low 32% Off High
RDN 32.81 26.4 37.34 9.56 24% Off Low 12% Off High
MUSA 479.46 380.95 561.08 10.05 26% Off Low 15% Off High
PAG 169.7 140.34 180.12 10.32 22% Off Low 5% Off High
RHI 60.4 56.16 81.31 22.65 8% Off Low 26% Off High
MCO 519.04 358.01 531.93 29.44 45% Off Low 2% Off High
SPGI 542.62 405.38 545.39 33.67 34% Off Low 1% Off High
AVA 37.42 31.51 39.24 20.94 19% Off Low 4% Off High
EQIX 939.08 673.78 994.03 136.86 39% Off Low 6% Off High
KNSL 440.64 354.77 547.93 81.3 25% Off Low 19% Off High
CSX 32.4 31.43 39.58 25.59 3% Off Low 18% Off High
BIP 33.26 23.91 36.08 0 39% Off Low 8% Off High
MGA 38.16 35.05 54.33 43.17 9% Off Low 30% Off High
WD 84.13 84.29 117.47 12.54 New Low 28% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the "Chowder Rule," the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
AVA 5.24 3.3 4 4.2 4.1 9.5
BIP 5.17 5.8 6 6.1 7.8 11.3
MGA 5.14 3.3 3.4 5.4 9.6 10.6
RHI 3.91 10.4 11.7 11.3 11.4 15.2
WD 3.19 3.2 9.1 16.7 19.8
RDN 3.17 8.9 21.6 150.2 58.2 153.4
PAG 2.88 47.1 32 21 18 23.8
BC 2.66 5 9.6 14.1 14.1 16.7
EQIX 2 17.6 14.1 11.6 13.6
CSX 1.6 9.1 8.8 8.5 8.6 9.9
MCO 0.72 10.4 11.1 11.2 11.8 11.9
SPGI 0.71 1.1 5.7 9.8 11.7 10.5
MUSA 0.41 15.5 19.9
KNSL 0.15 7.2 10.9 13.4 13.6

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market while also increasing their dividend payouts. I rely on two primary ETF benchmarks. If a stock does not outperform its relevant benchmark, I prefer to invest in an ETF instead.

I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) for broad access to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector. SCHD boasts a solid track record of strong performance, offers a higher yield than the S&P 500, and consistently raises dividends.

The ten-year dividend growth rate is significant and essential to the total return performance among the four key factors influencing the SCHD index. Share prices generally align with substantial dividend growth over extended periods, and long-term trends often outweigh short-term fluctuations. Consequently, I will exclude companies that lack a ten-year dividend growth rate from the upcoming comparison.

I prefer the Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (RNP) for REITs. It's a leveraged fund that combines common and preferred shares of REITs. The fund has an extensive track record since its launch in 2003 and has historically outperformed rivals such as the Vanguard Real Estate Index ETF (VNQ).

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I've added many companies to my portfolio and will make timely purchases of existing holdings. The chart will show the total return levels, assuming that dividends are reinvested.

I compare SCHD to the companies on this list with a 10-year dividend growth rate. This week, I can fit all of them in one chart.

About a third of the companies outperformed SCHD. SCHD, represented by the purple line, provided investors with a passive total return of 189% over the decade.

The group of outperformers consists of MCO, SPGI, PAG, and CSX. MCO and SPGI more than doubled the returns of SCHD, offering total returns of 491% and 472%, respectively. MCO, SPGI, and PAG also rank near the top of the list in terms of 10-year dividend growth, again, an indicator, though not a perfect predictor of 10-year returns.

The group of underperformers begins with BIP at 165% and continues down to MGA, with a 10-year performance of- 3%.

Next Steps

I hold high standards for the individual stocks in my portfolio. It's not just about slightly exceeding a benchmark; I look for companies with a strong track record of exceptional performance. I aim to carefully analyze their historical data to assess the likelihood of that success continuing.

I currently hold dividend king SPGI in my dividend growth portfolio. Given its high correlation with MCO, I don't see any point in looking at it further, and no one else on the list seems compelling to me.

Let me know what you think of my strategy, and feel free to add yours in the comments below! As always, please do your due diligence before making any investment decision.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Banco Bradesco's 16% Earnings Yield Is Not So Attractive Amid Its Challenges
data.description:
Banco Bradesco (NYSE:BBD) (NYSE:BBDO) reported 4Q24 results with very little to like in comparison to peers. The bank continues to lag behind peers in loan growth, NII lags loans, and expenses grow above NII and fees. Key retail categories like credit cards and current accounts are shrinking on a relative basis. For FY25, the expectation is for NII to expand, but expenses to grow above loans and fees.

Bradesco looks cheap because it is offering an earnings yield of 16%. However, on a comparative basis with peers (most of which trade cheaply), it is not so attractive. For this reason, I maintain a Hold on the bank (I started covering Bradesco with a Hold after 2Q24 earnings, and maintained the read after 3Q24 earnings).

4Q24 results

Loan Book Growth in Line with Market: Bradesco's loan book grew 12% YoY. This was in line with the expansion of the Brazilian credit market and most large banks. While this headline figure seems solid, the mix of loan growth was not optimal, favoring riskier segments and government-backed programs over high-margin retail lines

Loan segments are not the best: SMEs were the fastest-growing segment, up 28% year over year. While the bank emphasizes that much of this is tied to government-backed programs (FGI, FGO, Pronampe), SMEs are one of the riskiest areas of loans.

Conversely, payroll loans and credit cards, which typically carry higher spreads and generate better fees, lost relative weight in the portfolio.

NII and fees below loan growth: NII grew only 5.4% YoY before provisions, significantly lagging loan growth. This reflects ongoing compression in lending spreads. NII after NPLs grew much more, but only because FY23, and particularly 4Q23, had large provision recognitions from a significant deterioration in credit conditions in Brazil (Bradesco was probably the most hit of the large banks then).

Service fees grew 8% YoY, including the impact of the Cielo acquisition (a payments processor). Excluding Cielo, it was only 5%. Credit card fees, one of the highest-margin segments, grew only 2% in 2024 (excluding the Cielo effect), reinforcing Bradesco's underperformance in this area.

Expenses Growing Faster Than Revenues: OpEx climbed 9% YoY, faster than both NII and fees. Margins were higher YoY only because of large NPLs in 4Q23 and FY23. While broadly in line with other banks, this continues a negative trend where costs rise faster than revenue generation.

Valuation is not attractive on a relative basis

Bradesco generated R$20 billion in net income for 2024, or a 16% earnings yield based on its current market cap of $21.6 billion (R$123 billion).

This seems compelling and actually is. Bradesco is a large bank, with relatively stable operations, still growing, and yet offers that level of earnings yield.

In terms of risks, the bank shares approximately the same risks as its peers. First, its balance sheet is denominated in BRL and the currency could depreciate, damaging USD-denominated earnings power. Second, with interest rates growing, there could be a credit crunch in Brazil, leading to higher delinquencies (higher NPLs). The company's focus on SMEs makes this risk even higher.

In a sense, I believe these risks are well covered by the earnings yield.

However, compared to peers, the yield is not so good. Santander Brasil (BSBR), which is growing faster, innovating more, and operating more efficiently, trades at a 14% earnings yield. Banco do Brasil (OTCPK:BDORY), with superior profitability and execution, trades at similar or better levels.

Most financial institutions in Brazil are trading at low multiples, so this is not enough to recommend Bradesco. Bradesco is probably the most challenged in terms of lackluster operations, and although it may deserve a higher price, it is not so attractive compared to peers. Bradesco continues to underperform peers in key areas like loan mix, NII growth, service fee expansion, and cost control.

For that reason, I keep Bradesco as a Hold.

I would change my mind on the name under two scenarios: first, the bank shows a significant operational turnaround, with better credit, more revenue conversion, and better cost management. Second, its valuation gap to other Brazilian banks widens. Today, better Brazilian banks are trading at similar yields and, therefore, offering more opportunity.

data.title: Precision Drilling: Accelerating Capital Returns Should Put A Floor On The Stock
data.description:
All values are in CAD. Prices refer to the TSX traded stock and not the one on the US side.

In our inaugural coverage of Precision Drilling Corporation (TSX:PD:CA) (NYSE:PDS), we gave a brief history of the firm and went over why the current capital allocation plan had potential to deliver superb returns. Of course, the market decided that the company was still Precision Drilling and Quantum Drilling. This one actually had a 25% free cash flow yield today versus GAAP profitability based on 2040 estimates for Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR). So it made absolute sense for the market to say "meh" and deliver this for our buy thesis.

We review the Q4-2024 results and tell you why we expect our thesis to pan out even if we cannot convince the company to change its name to DeepSeek Drilling.

Q4-2024

While the company's overall trajectory remains excellent, Q4-2024 definitely showed a few soft spots. Revenues declined by 7.6% and the adjusted EBITDA missed estimates by a modest amount. Net capital spending was $50 million, a bit higher than where analysts expected.

The drilling market in the US came in softer than expected while Canada stayed relative resilient. You could see that in the stark contrast in the year over year delta in these two markets. Both drilling days and revenue per day moved in decidedly different directions. PDS also felt further pain as its operating costs on both sides of the 47th parallel rose year over year.

This reduced field margins to $14,559 per day, about 3% lower than their own guidance. In the face of these challenges, it was impressive that the company still delivered an overall solid EBITDA. But the analysts were quick to pounce on the miss and we saw a few downgrades and lower price targets post the quarter.

Outlook

PDS is not a get-rich-quick story. It is a longer-term deep value play where the company is working hard to fix the undervaluation. This begins with getting debt levels down.

As stated in their press release, they achieved exactly the midpoint of their 2024 target.

Reduce debt by between $150 million and $200 million and allocate 25% to 35% of free cash flow before debt repayments for share repurchases.

Reduced debt by $176 million and ended the year with a Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA ratio of approximately 1.4 times. On track to achieve a sustained Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA ratio of below 1.0 times.

Returned $75 million to shareholders through share repurchases, achieving the midpoint of our target range.

Renewed our NCIB in September, allowing repurchases of up to 10% of the public float.

Source: PDS Q4-2024 Press Release

The company announced its longer-term plans and the trajectory remains similar.

Reduce debt by at least $100 million in 2025 and debt by $700 million between 2022 and 2027, while remaining committed to achieving a sustained Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA ratio of below 1.0 times.

Allocate 35% to 45% of free cash flow, before debt repayments, directly to shareholders and continue moving direct shareholder capital returns toward 50% of free cash flow thereafter.

Source: PDS Q4-2024 Press Release

As we had previously stated, the initial round of buybacks have not materially moved the share count. That can be partially attributed to the figure below.

But reducing debt, reducing interest expense and a higher shareholder capital allocation, should change things. For 2025 the mean estimate is for about $300 million of free cash flow. We think this is a bit backward looking and focuses a lot on the Q4-2024 pain. Natural gas prices have improved in Q1-2025, thanks to persistent cold across North America. This should improve drilling at least modestly from here. Consensus is pricing a mild adjusted EBITDA decline. But let's run with their numbers and then knock of $50 million for stock-based compensation. We are still left with $250 million here relative to the following market capitalization.

The company should be able to return $125 million in the form of buybacks in 2025. This probably comes to an effective $80 million after accounting for stock-based compensation. The remaining cash would be directed towards debt reduction. So it might again be a bit from a stock price movement perspective. After all, something trading this cheap can get cheaper. But the numbers get really good once you go past 2025 and will likely get incredible if we have a genuine drilling up cycle. We could easily see $400 million of free cash flow in a peak year. Valuation can really swing and give you phenomenal returns. Imagine a 5-10X free cash flow multiple at the peak, and account for the reduced share count over the next two years.

Verdict

PDS is not the most efficient user of assets and that is partially the reason for the multiples relative to Nabors Industries (NBR) and Patterson-UTI Energy Inc. (PTEN).

But the discount here is phenomenal from multiple angles. You have the massive free cash flow yield which goes more and more towards shareholder returns over time. But even from sheer worth of these assets, you are getting a bargain. Realistically, we think tangible book value understates replacement cost by about 20%-30%. This is because real loss of value is far less than booked depreciation. So a true NAV like metric here would be closer to $150. The stock is trading at close to 2.5X EV to EBITDA. In a world where 50x sales seems to be a "bargain", one cannot rule out further value compression in actual value stocks. But we are sticking with this and adding positions married to December 2025 covered calls.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Celsius Holdings: Stock Surged 30% Post Earnings, Deep Dive Into The Numbers
data.description:
Introduction

Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) announced its Q4 2024 earnings report today after the close. In the after-hour trading session, the stock first sold off quite substantially, down 9%. This was after The Wall Street Journal spread the story that CELH was buying its rival “Alani Nu” for $1.8 billion. Shortly after this, CELH presented earnings and made public that they were indeed buying Alani Nu. The stock is currently up by 30%, just a few minutes after the earnings release.

I will give you a brief look over the earnings and my opinion on them.

Q4 2024 earnings report

Solid 2024 growth, even though the last quarter saw increased pressure due to declining sales in North America.

Financial highlights of the fourth quarter:

CELH reported $332.2 million in sales, which are down 4% compared to the same quarter in the previous year. This is mainly due to North American sales being down 6% YOY. With $311.9 million, the North American region represents the major source of revenue. Contrary to this is CELH's international business, which increased by 39% YOY to $20.3 million. This was done via the expansion into the UK, Ireland, France, Australia, and New Zealand.

Thanks to lower freight and material costs, the gross margin improved by 240 basis points to 50.2%. Contrary to this, net income decreased by a whopping -138% to -$18.9 million. According to CELH, this stems from high legal expenses, restructuring costs, and co-packing obligations.

  • Revenue: $332.2 million (-4%)
  • Gross Margin: 50.2% (+240 basis points)
  • Net Income: -$18.9 million (-138%)
  • SG&A Expenses: $185.2 million (+73%)
  • Diluted EPS: -$0.11 (previous year: $0.17).

Full-Year 2024: Moderate Growth Despite Challenges

Even though the fourth quarter dragged sales growth down, CELH managed to achieve 3% revenue growth, reaching $1.36 billion for the full year. International sales were the main driver with a 37% increase for 2024. However, net income declined by 36% to $145.1 million, and diluted EPS fell 42% to $0.45. According to the management, investments in vertical integration, new market expansion, and strategic initiatives impacted short-term profitability but will drive long-term growth.

Key Figures (2024 vs. 2023):

  • Revenue: $1.36 billion (+3%)
  • International Revenue: $74.7 million (+37%)
  • Gross Margin: 50.2% (+220 basis points)
  • Net Income: $145.1 million (-36%)
  • Diluted EPS: $0.45 (-42%)
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $255.7 million (-13%).

Market Performance

While Celsius’ U.S. retail sales grew by 22% for the year, the company experienced a slight decline in market share in Q4 (-0.5% to 10.9%). Nevertheless, for the full year, Celsius' market share increased by 1.6% to 11.8%.

CEO John Fieldly highlighted the company’s strong brand momentum and long-term potential:

”Our record $1.36 billion revenue underscores the strength of our brand. With the Alani Nu acquisition and continued expansion, we are well-positioned for sustained growth.”

CFO Jarrod Langhans added:

”Our financial strategy enables us to balance sustainable growth with profitable expansion. Investments in vertical integration and international markets provide long-term competitive advantages.”

Celsius Holdings: 2025 Outlook – Growth, Expansion & Strategic Moves

According to management, Celsius Holdings is ready for strong growth despite short-term challenges. Focus areas for 2025 are international expansion, product innovation, retail growth, and the Alani Nu acquisition.

Because the outlook and strategies are quite extensive, I split them in different segments to give you a better overview.

Alani Nu Acquisition: A Key Growth Driver

The $1.65 billion acquisition of Alani Nu is expected to:

  • Expand market share with strong Gen Z and millennial appeal
  • Contribute $595 million in additional revenue
  • Generate $50 million in annual cost synergies
  • Strengthen presence in functional lifestyle products.

Goal: $2 billion+ in revenue through brand integration.

International Expansion: Scaling Global Presence

  • +37% international sales in 2024 – major focus area for 2025
  • Leverage PepsiCo’s distribution network for faster global scaling
  • Targeted growth regions: Asia-Pacific, Europe.

Market potential: Global energy drinks sector to grow 10% CAGR (2024-2029).

Product Innovation & Portfolio Expansion

Consumer demand for health-conscious and functional beverages is rising. Celsius plans to:

  • Launch new product categories: hydration, protein-based drinks
  • Introduce limited-edition flavors to increase consumer engagement
  • Expand into new functional wellness segments.

Goal: Stay ahead of market trends and reinforce brand loyalty.

Margin Expansion & Profitability Improvements

Despite higher SG&A expenses, Celsius aims to:

  • Optimize supply chains for lower material & freight costs
  • Enhance economies of scale through expanded production
  • Improve direct-to-consumer (DTC) sales for higher margins.

Target: Maintain 50%+ gross margin while investing in growth.

Retail & Distribution Growth in North America

  • 37% increase in retail distribution points in 2024
  • Strengthen partnerships with Walmart, Target, and major retailers
  • Expand into fitness centers & health-focused outlets
  • Boost marketing and brand visibility to regain sales momentum.

Objective: Regain U.S. sales growth despite the Q4 slowdown.

Financial Strategy & Liquidity Management

  • $890 million in cash reserves ensures flexibility
  • Low net leverage (~1.0x) even after Alani Nu deal
  • Balanced capital allocation: Growth investments + debt reduction.

Plan: Maintain financial strength while pursuing aggressive expansion.

2025 Key Targets

  • $2 billion+ total revenue
  • 50 million in cost synergies from the Alani Nu integration
  • Double-digit international sales growth
  • Improved gross margin via operational efficiencies
  • Expanded retail footprint & new product launches.

Previous financial performance

CELH’s recent quarterly numbers look nearly perfect. Revenue, EPS, and free cash flow have all grown substantially since 2022, and they managed to become comfortably profitable. The last quarter presents an exception, as you can see in the following chart:

As with everything in the market, there are always ups and downs; occasional bumps are normal. What matters is the long-term trend. And I would argue that the long-term trend looks good. In my opinion, the recent earnings have shown that the long-term growth path is secured.

Valuation

Currently, CELH is valued at 32x forward earnings, far below its three-year average and near its lowest historical valuation.

For a company with CELH’s growth, market position, total addressable market (TAM), and excellent management, I believe this valuation is justified.

After the very positive earnings price reaction, the valuation will be higher. But this is the forward P/E, and I am sure analysts will raise their estimates, lowering the valuation again. In any case, it will still be way above its previous fwd P/E average.

Risks

The major risk I see is that inflation is further accelerating. For CELH this would create two major concerns:

  • higher inflation data could force the FED to raise rates again, which would increase the cost of financing, which hits smaller growth companies the hardest.
  • accelerating inflation means an even higher cost of living, which is already a problem for most people, especially in the US. CELH sells something that is not necessary and could and will be ditched by consumers if they have to choose between things they need and CELH's energy drinks.

CELH’s management has not addressed this, but I see it as a risk.

Conclusion

CELH is entering a pivotal phase in its growth journey. Short-term profitability challenges exist, but the company’s strong brand momentum, global expansion, and strategic acquisitions position it for accelerated growth in 2025 and beyond. CELH’s management has proven that they can steer the company toward growth and are among the top in the industry. While there are risks and short-term profitability challenges, I am sure that the current stock prices will be a steal compared to the prices you will have to pay soon. Therefore, I rate this stock as a buy.
